The dashboard must expose a coverage indicator and enforce data quality checks before broad rollout.

Minimum checks:
* Completeness: hourly rows present for required dimensions (model, region, tier) above threshold.
* Streaming parity: output tokens for streaming paths are not systematically undercounted vs non-streaming.
* Cardinality guardrails: quantization_profile, model_version values do not explode beyond expected controlled vocab.
* “Unknown” bucket monitoring: unknown region/tier/model must remain below agreed threshold.

h3. SLOs (initial targets)
(Owned by SRE/Telemetry; see SLO doc for final numbers. v1 targets below are used for launch gating.)
* Freshness: 95% of hours available within 2 hours of hour-end.
* Completeness: for each hour, >= 99% of output tokens have non-null canonical model + region + tier.
* Correctness: automated checks show streaming/non-streaming token parity within agreed tolerance over 7d windows.

h3. Incident response
* Alerts route to Eng Platform/SRE on-call, with escalation to Telemetry/Analytics and Finance when reconciliation is affected.
* Runbook must include validation queries, backfill procedure, and comms template for Finance.

h2. Success Metrics
h3. Adoption (internal)
* Weekly Active Viewers (WAV):
  * Week 2 after internal dogfood: >= 15 unique viewers
  * By v1 launch + 4 weeks: >= 40 unique viewers across Finance/Product/Eng
* Finance usage: at least 1 recurring weekly Finance review that uses the dashboard as the source of truth for QTD unit cost.

h3. Effectiveness
* Time-to-diagnosis: reduce median time to identify top driver of a unit-cost change from >1 day to <2 hours (measured via internal survey + incident notes).
* Regression detection: catch at least 2 unit-cost regressions via dashboard signals/alerts within the first 60 days post-launch.

h3. Data trust
* Finance signoff achieved (methodology + reconciliation) before enabling the dashboard beyond the initial allowlist.
* SLO compliance: meet freshness/completeness SLOs for 14 consecutive days post-launch.

h2. Scope (v1)
Included:
* Canonical metric definition and warehouse views/tables
* Hourly aggregation job + backfill tooling for 2025 Q1
* Console APIs for time series and breakdowns (Top-N)
* Console internal UI (filters, charts, breakdown tables, drilldowns)
* Alerts, SLO dashboards, runbooks, and access controls

Excluded:
* Per-customer drilldowns
* Revenue/margin views (cost-only unit metric)
* Fully real-time streaming dashboards

h2. Milestones and Rollout Plan
h3. Phase 0 — Definition + alignment (Weeks 1–2)
Deliverables:
* Finance-aligned metric definition and methodology references
* Dimension definitions (model/version/quant, region normalization, tier mapping)
* Gap analysis (streaming parity, missing dims)
Exit criteria:
* Rishi (FP&A) + Laura (CFO) approve the methodology draft for implementation

h3. Phase 1 — Telemetry + aggregation (Weeks 3–6)
Deliverables:
* Streaming output token metering parity fixes
* Hourly aggregation tables + initial warehouse views
* Reconciliation prototype report
Exit criteria:
* 7-day backtest with stable results; unknown-dimension rates within thresholds

h3. Phase 2 — Console API + UI (Weeks 5–8)
Deliverables:
* Query endpoints (time series + breakdowns + drilldowns)
* Console internal page with filters + top-N tables + drilldowns
Exit criteria:
* Dogfood with Product/Eng; performance targets met (p95 dashboard query < 2s for default views)

h3. Phase 3 — Hardening + access controls (Weeks 8–10)
Deliverables:
* SLO dashboards/alerts wired to on-call
* Runbook and escalation policy
* RBAC + feature flag + audit logging
Exit criteria:
* SLOs met for at least 7 consecutive days in dogfood

h3. Phase 4 — Staged rollout + exec readiness (Weeks 10–12)
Rollout steps:
1) Expand allowlist to Finance + Product leads + Eng managers
2) Weekly reconciliation check with Finance for two cycles
3) Publish internal announcement + feedback channel

Go/No-Go criteria:
* Finance signoff complete
* Reconciliation report shows agreed tolerance vs Finance totals for QTD
* Alerts tested; runbook validated by on-call

h2. Risks and Mitigations
*Risk:* Streaming output token undercount leads to inflated blended cost.
*Mitigation:* Parity tests + canary checks; explicit dashboard warning if parity check fails.

*Risk:* Tier mapping ambiguity (resellers, pilots, credits) makes tier drilldowns untrustworthy.
*Mitigation:* Define authoritative tier source; default to “Unknown” with alerting if mapping coverage drops.

*Risk:* Region normalization mismatches between billing/infrastructure labels and request telemetry.
*Mitigation:* Canonical mapping table with versioning; monitor “unknown region” bucket; backfill mechanism.

*Risk:* High-cardinality tags (model version / quant profile) degrade metrics stability and cost.
*Mitigation:* Allowlisted values + validation at ingestion; periodic audits.

*Risk:* Misinterpretation of blended cost as marginal cost by stakeholders.
*Mitigation:* UI copy/tooltips + documentation; separate “live” vs “finance reporting” view.

h2. Open Questions
1) Idle capacity allocation: confirm final treatment and how it’s explained in the UI (tooltip text and examples). Owner: Rishi Malhotra.
2) Overhead allocation: which overhead categories (if any) are included in v1 blended cost. Owner: Laura Bennett.
3) Thresholding/redaction: what minimum volume thresholds should be enforced to avoid re-identification risk. Owners: Marcus Lin + Finance.
4) “Finance reporting” view definition: what constitutes “stable” (rate card version lock; backfills allowed; revision policy). Owners: Finance + Telemetry.
5) Prefill/decode ratio availability: confirm instrumentation readiness and whether it ships in v1 or v1.1. Owner: Nadia Rahman.

h2. Appendix: Acceptance Criteria (v1)
* The dashboard displays blended $/1M output tokens with correct units and consistent definitions.
* Users can filter by time, model, region, customer tier, and drill down to model version and quantization profile.
* Top-N tables for models/regions/tiers match the time series totals (within rounding).
* Data quality indicator reflects missingness and updates as checks fail/pass.
* Access is internal-only with RBAC and audit logging.
* Finance can use QTD view for KPI reporting with documented reconciliation and rate card provenance.

h1. Internal Unit Economics Dashboard (v1) — Product Requirements Document

h2. Summary
Redwood Inference will build an internal-only “Unit Economics” dashboard in the Redwood Console to make inference cost drivers visible and actionable across Product, Engineering, and Finance.

The dashboard’s primary KPI is *Blended $/1M output tokens*, with drilldowns by:
* Model (including model version and quantization profile)
* Region (canonical normalized region)
* Customer tier (Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise)

The dashboard is intended to support Cost Optimization Automation efforts (batching, caching, quantization) by:
1) quantifying impact, 2) identifying regressions, and 3) enabling prioritization of runtime/infra work.

h2. Goals (v1)
* Establish a *trusted, finance-aligned* definition of “Blended $/1M output tokens” that reconciles with infra spend inputs and platform metering.
* Enable interactive drilldowns and comparisons to identify unit-cost outliers by model/region/tier.
* Surface contributing signals to support diagnosis: cache hit rate, batching efficiency, average output length, streaming vs non-streaming, and quantization profile.
* Operationalize metric reliability via data quality checks, SLOs, alerts, and an on-call playbook so the KPI is dependable for exec reporting.

h2. Non-goals (explicit, v1)
* External/customer-facing cost dashboards.
* Fully automated optimization actions (the dashboard provides measurement/diagnostics that feed automation).
* Per-customer or per-request identification in the UI (avoid customer-identifiable drilldowns; only aggregated views).
* Real-time cost reporting (v1 targets hourly aggregation and near-real-time freshness, not second-level).

h2. Personas + Use Cases
h3. Finance (CFO / FP&A)
* Primary users: Laura Bennett (CFO), Rishi Malhotra (FP&A)
* Use cases:
  * Quarter-to-date / month-to-date unit cost trending for exec reporting.
  * Reconciliation of KPI vs infra spend (rate card) and token metering.
  * Understand how mix shifts (region/model/tier) change blended unit cost.

h3. Engineering (Telemetry/Analytics, Serving Runtime, Infra)
* Primary users: Logan Wright (Telemetry/Analytics), Nadia Rahman (Token metering), Sean Gallagher (SRE)
* Use cases:
  * Detect regressions (e.g., output token undercounting, region mapping gaps, sudden cost spikes).
  * Attribute cost changes to signals (cache hit rate, batching utilization, quant profile changes).
  * Verify improvements from runtime changes and infrastructure optimizations.

h3. Product (Console, Optimize)
* Primary users: Marissa Cole (Console), Aditya Rao (Optimize)
* Use cases:
  * Prioritize optimization roadmap based on largest cost opportunities.
  * Validate that optimization launches reduced blended cost without hidden regressions.
  * Communicate cost drivers to GTM/CS with appropriate caveats and aggregation.

h2. Key Definitions (v1)
This section defines the canonical metric as displayed in the dashboard. For full methodology and reconciliation rules, see “Blended Cost Methodology v1” and ADR-034.

h3. KPI: Blended $/1M Output Tokens
*Display name in Console:* “Blended cost ($/1M output tokens)”

*Definition (high level):*
Blended $/1M output tokens = (Allocated inference cost in USD / Total output tokens) × 1,000,000

h4. Numerator: Allocated inference cost (USD)
Cost is computed from a finance-owned GPU hourly rate card and allocated to usage based on the adopted attribution model (ADR-034). v1 includes:
* GPU compute costs (fleet spend applied via hourly rate card)
* Region/provider-specific rates (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
* Capacity-type handling (hosted shared pools vs reserved/dedicated pools)
* Idle capacity treatment per ADR-034 (v1 will display *blended* cost, inclusive of allocated idle, not marginal)

Explicit exclusions in v1 (unless specified by Finance methodology):
* Non-GPU costs (e.g., general SaaS tools) unless included as a fixed overhead allocation in the finance methodology
* One-time credits/promotions (these affect revenue/billing, not cost)

h4. Denominator: Output tokens
*Output tokens* are the platform-metered tokens generated by the model as completion output.

Inclusions:
* Tokens produced in non-streaming responses
* Tokens produced in streaming responses (requires parity across streaming code paths)

Exclusions/notes:
* Input/prompt tokens are *not* in the denominator for this KPI (v1 is output-token-centric for comparability across workloads).
* Tool call payload tokens are included only if they are counted by the canonical tokenizer in the serving path; v1 will follow metering standard and document known edge cases.
* Truncation behavior must be consistent: count tokens actually emitted to the client (or logically generated if streaming partial emissions are retried), per metering spec.

h4. Dimensions (grouping keys)
All drilldowns and filters must use canonical normalized dimensions to keep results stable.

Required dimensions in v1:
* Model identifier (canonical): model_family + model_name + model_version
* Quantization profile: controlled vocabulary (e.g., fp16, int8, w4a16) with guardrails against high-cardinality tags
* Region: canonical region mapping table (normalize cloud regions/internal labels)
* Customer tier: Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise derived from authoritative sources (billing plan + contract flags + routing context)

Optional dimensions (not first-class in v1 UI, but may exist in the warehouse):
* Route, endpoint, streaming flag, request class

h3. “Blended” vs “Marginal” cost (user-facing guidance)
The v1 KPI is *blended*, not marginal.
* Blended includes allocated shared overhead and idle per ADR-034.
* Users must not interpret short-window changes as purely causal; the dashboard will include tooltip guidance and (where available) a confidence/coverage indicator.

h2. User Experience Requirements (v1)
(See UX spec for layouts; this PRD defines functional requirements.)

h3. Entry points
* Redwood Console (internal-only) → “Unit Economics” page under internal tooling section.
* Direct deep link URLs must preserve filter state for sharing internally.

h3. Default view

* Time range: Last 7 days
* Grouping: Overall (no grouping) with a supporting breakdown (Top models)
* Customer tier: All
* Region: All
* Model: All

The dashboard must prominently show:
* KPI value for selected time range
* Time series chart (hourly or daily aggregation, depending on range)
* Top-N breakdown tables (models, regions, tiers)
* Coverage/data-quality indicator (see Data Quality)

h3. Filters (must-have)
* Time range selector with presets: 24h, 7d, 30d, QTD, custom
* Model selector (supports model family/name; advanced toggle for model version)
* Region selector (canonical regions)
* Customer tier selector (self-serve/dedicated/private/enterprise)

h3. Drilldowns
v1 drilldown behavior:
* Clicking a Top-N row navigates to a drilldown view with that dimension pinned and secondary breakdowns available.
* Drilldowns supported in v1:
  * Model → Model version → Quantization profile
  * Region → (optional) Provider/capacity type if available in warehouse
  * Customer tier → (no deeper drilldown by customer)

h3. Contributing signals (diagnostic overlays)
The dashboard must display (at minimum) the following signals alongside cost to support diagnosis:
* Cache hit rate (prefix/KV cache) — aggregated
* Batching efficiency/utilization — aggregated
* Avg output length (tokens/output) — aggregated
* Streaming share (% requests streaming)
* Prefill/decode ratio (if available by v1; otherwise mark as v1.1)

Each signal must have:
* A clear definition tooltip
* A statement of known limitations (e.g., only available for certain runtimes/regions)

h2. Functional Requirements
h3. Computation and freshness
* Aggregation cadence: hourly computation with availability in Console within defined freshness SLO.
* Support daily rollups for longer ranges (e.g., > 30 days) to keep queries fast.
* Provide two “views” of freshness in v1:
  * “Live” view (fast updates, may be preliminary)
  * “Finance reporting” view (stable, reconciled, uses versioned rate card and allocation rules)

h3. Reconciliation
* Provide a reconciliation report/view that compares:
  * Total allocated cost (USD) for period
  * Total output tokens for period
  * Derived blended $/1M output tokens
  * Differences vs finance reporting totals (with explained deltas)

Acceptance criteria for Finance signoff (v1):
* QTD blended $/1M output tokens matches Finance’s methodology within an agreed tolerance for at least 2 consecutive weekly checks.
* Rate card provenance and version used for each period is visible (metadata).

h3. Access control and auditability
* Internal-only feature flag gate.
* RBAC: allowlist initial groups (Finance, Exec staff, Eng leads for telemetry/runtime, Product Console/Optimize).
* Audit log access (who viewed the page and when) is required because the metric is sensitive.

h3. Privacy and safe aggregation
* No customer-identifiable data in tables or drilldowns.
* No raw request IDs.
* Minimum thresholding: suppress rows with extremely low volume (tokens or cost) if it increases re-identification risk. Threshold values to be set with Security + Finance.

h2. Data Quality and Reliability Requirements
h3. Data quality gates (v1)

  * By v1 launch + 4 weeks: >= 40 unique viewers across Finance/Product/Eng
* Finance usage: at least 1 recurring weekly Finance review that uses the dashboard as the source of truth for QTD unit cost.

h3. Effectiveness

* Regression detection: catch at least 2 unit-cost regressions via dashboard signals/alerts within the first 60 days post-launch.

source.title: Hosted API graceful degradation: automatic fallback routing
source.author: Maya Srinivasan
source.content:
# Overview
This document specifies how Redwood Hosted API will **automatically fall back** during partial outages and capacity shortfalls. The goal is to reduce customer-visible errors/timeouts without violating contract constraints (data residency, pinning) or feature constraints (tool calling, structured output).

This is a *completeness* spec for all forms of Hosted API auto-fallback.

# User-facing behavior
When a request cannot be served on the primary route, the router will select a fallback route and proceed without requiring retries from the customer. Customers may observe:
- Same response schema as the primary route (OpenAI-compatible endpoints included).
- Potentially different latency characteristics.
- Optional response header `x-redwood-fallback: <reason_code>` in v2 (behind feature flag).

# In-scope endpoints
- `POST /v1/chat/completions` (streaming + non-streaming)
- `POST /v1/completions`
- `POST /v1/responses` (new unified endpoint)

# Fallback types
## 1) Region fallback
- Only between regions approved for the account (e.g., `us-east` → `us-west`).
- Must preserve data residency constraints.

## 2) Model-variant fallback
- Allows fallback to a compatible variant (e.g., `rw-llama3-70b` → `rw-llama3-70b-int8`).
- Must preserve required capabilities:
  - tool calling
  - JSON schema / structured output
  - max context length requirement
  - safety policy compatibility

# Primary reasons for fallback
- Route unhealthy (5xx/timeout above threshold).
- Capacity throttling from serving cluster (scheduler rejects / queue saturation).
- Latency SLO violation sustained (guarded by capacity utilization).

# Constraints & guardrails
- **Model pinning**: if customer pins a specific model version/build, only region fallback is allowed.
- **Enterprise allowlists**: some enterprise accounts must not use quantized variants; FPE must respect account allowlist.
- **Auditability**: every fallback decision must be traceable in logs and queryable for support.

# Config model (high level)
- Router config includes `fallback_routes` per primary route.
- Feature flag: `router_fpe_enabled` (account-scoped) and `router_fpe_headers` (global).

# Success metrics
- Reduce customer-visible 5xx during capacity incidents by 50% (baseline: Nov 2024 incidents).
- Reduce manual failover interventions by 80%.

# Open questions
- Should we expose fallback events in Console usage analytics? (tentative: yes, as aggregated counts)
- Should customers be able to opt out? (tentative: opt-out for enterprise only)

# Related
- ADR-0142: Fallback Policy Engine (FPE)
- SUP playbook updates (runbook + known issues)

source.title: PRD: Dedicated Burst Capacity (Controlled Burst Option)
source.author: Lauren Bishop
source.body:
h1. Summary

Dedicated Burst Capacity is an add-on option for Redwood Dedicated customers that allows *temporary throughput above a tenant’s reserved (baseline) GPU allocation* by borrowing from Redwood-managed *burst pools* (shared, segmented by region and hardware class). Bursting is strictly controlled via admission control, fairness enforcement, cooldown windows, and fleet-wide circuit breakers to prevent noisy-neighbor effects and protect platform-wide SLOs.

This PRD defines the customer problem, user stories, requirements, non-goals, SLOs, pricing assumptions, rollout plan, and success metrics.

h1. Problem statement

Dedicated customers routinely experience predictable peaks (e.g., batch re-indexing, weekly traffic spikes, product launches). Today, they must either:

* Over-provision baseline Dedicated capacity (wasting cost most of the time), or
* Accept degraded performance and/or hard throttling during peaks.

Customers want a way to *pay for occasional headroom* without permanently increasing committed baseline capacity, while still receiving a “Dedicated-grade” experience (reliable, isolated, supportable).

h1. Goals

* Provide a *paid* burst option for Dedicated customers that increases throughput during predictable peaks without requiring permanent over-provisioning.
* Protect non-bursting tenants and the broader fleet from noisy-neighbor effects via strict admission control, fairness, and circuit breakers.
* Make bursting observable, auditable, and supportable:
  * customer-visible reporting and Console surfaces
  * clear denial reasons
  * SRE alerts and operational runbooks
* Integrate with billing and packaging:
  * accurate burst metering
  * configurable per-tenant limits
  * contract/SLA language that sets correct expectations

h1. Non-goals (initial release)

* Cross-region bursting (burst capacity is constrained within a region).
* Customer-custom burst pools (initially shared Redwood-managed pools with segmentation).
* Spot-market behavior, auction pricing, or reservation trading.
* Guaranteeing burst availability at all times (burst is “best-effort within guardrails,” with clear limits and denial semantics).

h1. Definitions

* *Baseline (Reserved) Capacity:* Dedicated GPUs committed to the tenant (contracted, reserved pools).
* *Burst Capacity:* Additional capacity above baseline, sourced from Redwood-managed burst pools.
* *Burst Pool:* A shared pool of GPUs segmented by (region, hardware class) with fleet safety caps.
* *Burst Window:* The time interval during which a tenant is actively consuming burst capacity.
* *Admission Controller:* Data-plane-adjacent service that evaluates whether a request can consume burst capacity now, returning an admit/deny decision with reason.

h1. Personas

* *ML/Platform Engineer (Customer):* owns throughput and latency; needs predictable scaling behavior and clear operational knobs.
* *SRE/On-call (Redwood):* needs guardrails, alerts, and safe rollback to protect non-burst SLOs.
* *Account Executive / Solutions Engineer:* needs a crisp story for “burst vs increasing baseline,” with packaging boundaries and expectation setting.
* *Finance/Procurement (Customer):* wants a clear bill line item, predictable caps, and auditability.

h1. Key user stories

1) *Burst enablement and limits*
* As a Dedicated customer admin, I can enable burst for my tenant and set a maximum burst limit so we can safely handle peaks without runaway spend.

2) *Peak throughput handling*
* As an application owner, during a predictable spike, I can exceed baseline throughput while still receiving clear feedback if burst is unavailable or capped.

3) *Operational visibility*
* As a platform engineer, I can see burst usage over time, current limit state, denial reasons, and latency impact so I can tune my client behavior and capacity strategy.

4) *Redwood safety and support*
* As Redwood SRE, I can detect burst pool saturation and neighbor impact early, and quickly mitigate via circuit breakers/limit reductions with auditable actions.

5) *Billing clarity*
* As customer finance, I can reconcile burst charges to measured burst usage, with no double counting on retries.

h1. Product requirements

h2. Functional requirements

h3. Burst pool model

* Burst pools are provisioned and managed by Redwood by:
  * region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
  * hardware class (e.g., H100, L40S)
* A Dedicated tenant may burst only into pools that match its allowed region/hardware class.
* Burst pools must support logical segmentation to reduce correlated failures and prevent “one big shared pool” risk.

h3. Tenant configuration

The control plane must support per-tenant burst configuration:

|| Field || Description || Example ||
| burst_enabled | Master enablement toggle | true |
| max_burst_ratio | Maximum burst relative to baseline (cap) | 1.5 (i.e., up to +50% of baseline) |
| max_burst_gpus | Absolute cap for burst GPUs (optional override) | 8 |
| burst_cooldown_seconds | Cooldown after sustained bursting or after cap reached | 900 |
| priority_tier | Admission priority tier for fairness weights | silver |
| allowed_regions | Burst allowed regions (subset) | [us-east] |
| allowed_hardware_classes | Allowed hardware classes | [H100] |

Validation requirements:
* Must not allow burst caps that exceed fleet safety policy by default.
* Safe defaults: burst disabled unless explicitly enabled.
* Changes are auditable (see Audit Logging).

h3. Admission control & denial semantics

* Every request that would exceed baseline capacity must be evaluated for burst admission.
* Admission decisions must be *deterministic for a given request context* to avoid oscillation (within the limits of telemetry freshness).
* Denial must return a *machine-readable reason code* and a recommended client action.

Denial behaviors (initial release):
* Immediate deny with backpressure response for synchronous API traffic.
* Optional bounded queueing may be enabled for specific workloads later; not required for GA.

h3. Fairness and anti-starvation

* Admission must enforce fairness across bursting tenants within the same pool.
* Must include anti-starvation protections so one high-priority tenant cannot permanently suppress others.
* Must provide SRE-visible metrics that quantify fairness outcomes (e.g., per-tenant admitted share vs configured weights).

h3. Cooldown and burst shaping

* Enforce a cooldown window to prevent repeated “burst hammering” that destabilizes pools.
* Allow a configured “ramp-up” behavior (soft start) to reduce sudden tail latency amplification.

h3. Routing and fallback behavior

When burst is denied:
* Routing must not silently push the request into other tenants’ baseline capacity.
* Supported actions (policy-based):
  1) Return backpressure (429 / overload) with reason + retry hints
  2) Route to baseline-only path (slower/queued) if customer has chosen this mode
  3) Fallback to compatible model variant within the tenant’s baseline (only if configured by customer and compatible with their quality requirements)

h3. Metering and billing hooks

* Meter burst usage accurately and export to billing pipeline.
* Must be idempotent on retries (no double counting).
* Provide per-tenant daily summary exports for CS and customers.

h3. Console / customer experience

In Redwood Console for Dedicated tenants:
* Show current burst status (enabled/disabled), configured limits, and priority tier.
* Show usage charts:
  * baseline utilization vs burst utilization
  * burst “headroom remaining” indicators
* Show denial breakdown:
  * by reason code
  * over time
* Provide guidance text:
  * what to do when denied
  * recommended retry/backoff patterns

h3. Audit logging

* Log all burst configuration changes, including:
  * enable/disable
  * limit changes
  * priority tier changes
  * admin overrides / emergency actions
* Audit entries must include actor, timestamp, before/after, and justification (for manual overrides).

h2. Non-functional requirements

h3. Reliability / safety

* Primary safety requirement: *do not regress non-burst tenants’ SLOs.*
* Must implement fleet-wide safety caps and a global circuit breaker that can rapidly reduce/disable burst.
* Must support staged rollout and fast rollback.

h3. Performance

* Admission check must add minimal overhead. Target: p99 admission evaluation time <= 5 ms in-region.
* Telemetry lag must not cause systematic unfair denials; system must behave safely under partial telemetry outage.

h3. Security

* RBAC: only authorized roles can modify burst configuration.
* Debug tooling must redact sensitive identifiers when used outside trusted contexts.

h3. Operability

* On-call must have:
  * dashboards for saturation, denials, latency deltas
  * clear runbooks
  * tooling to explain denial decisions for a given request/tenant

h1. SLOs and SLAs

h2. What is protected

For GA, Redwood protects the *baseline Dedicated experience*.

* Baseline capacity SLOs remain the primary commitment.
* Burst capacity is provided on a controlled, guardrailed basis and may be denied to protect fleet safety and neighbor SLOs.

h2. Proposed SLO targets (initial)

|| Metric || Applies to || Target || Notes ||
| Dedicated baseline availability | Dedicated tenants | >= 99.9% monthly | Existing Dedicated SLO; bursting must not degrade it |
| Dedicated baseline latency | Dedicated tenants | No regression vs pre-burst baseline | Enforced via SLO regression gate |
| Burst admission service availability | Burst-enabled tenants | >= 99.9% monthly | If admission service is unhealthy, default to safe deny/disable burst |
| Burst admission overhead | Burst path | p99 <= 5 ms | Measured at gateway/routing layer |

h2. Guardrail SLO gates

* If non-burst tenant p95 latency in a pool increases by more than agreed threshold (see design spec; initial: +10% sustained for 10 minutes) AND correlates with burst saturation, automatically:
  * reduce max burst ratio globally for that pool, or
  * disable burst for that pool (circuit breaker)
* Error budget gates for rollout:
  * do not expand rollout if baseline SLO burn rate exceeds policy thresholds

h1. Packaging & pricing assumptions (for PRD; final owned by Pricing/Packaging doc)

Note: numbers here are placeholders for modeling and UX; final pricing requires Finance approval and GTM alignment.

h2. Packaging proposal

* Burst is an add-on for Dedicated contracts.
* Default availability: selected regions/hardware classes at GA.
* Priority tiers (example):
  * *Silver:* standard fairness weight, standard caps
  * *Gold:* higher fairness weight within strict anti-starvation constraints

h2. Metering unit

* Bill burst usage as *burst GPU-seconds* (or equivalent normalized compute-seconds) attributable to burst-only work.
* Secondary reporting for customer comprehension:
  * burst tokens generated
  * burst request counts

h2. Pricing assumptions

* Burst usage is charged at a premium vs baseline committed rate to reflect on-demand flexibility and fleet risk.
* Contracts include:
  * a maximum burst cap (ratio and/or absolute GPUs)
  * a statement that burst is subject to admission control and may be denied to protect fleet safety

h1. UX and API behavior

h2. Customer-visible responses when burst is denied

When burst is denied, customers must receive:
* HTTP status: 429 (Overloaded) or equivalent backpressure error
* Response body includes:
  * reason_code
  * retry_after_ms (when applicable)
  * guidance string (short)

Initial reason codes (must be stable and documented):

|| reason_code || Meaning || Recommended client behavior ||
| BURST_DISABLED | Burst not enabled for tenant/region/hw | Use baseline capacity; request config change if needed |
| TENANT_BURST_CAP_REACHED | Tenant hit configured burst cap | Backoff; reduce concurrency; wait for cooldown |
| POOL_SATURATED | Pool at or above safe utilization | Exponential backoff + jitter; consider baseline increase |
| FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN | Global breaker open for pool | Retry with backoff; expect limited availability until cleared |
| TELEMETRY_STALE_SAFE_DENY | Telemetry too stale; safe deny | Retry with backoff; Redwood investigating if sustained |
| POLICY_VALIDATION_FAILED | Misconfig (invalid limits/region mismatch) | Contact Support; fix config |

h1. Observability requirements

h2. Customer-facing

Console dashboards must include:
* Burst usage over time (GPU-seconds, tokens, requests)
* Current configured cap vs current estimated consumption
* Denial reasons over time
* “Burst impact” indicators (customer-visible but carefully framed):
  * p95 latency (baseline vs during burst windows)
  * admission latency

h2. Internal SRE/Engineering

Required metrics (examples; final names in metrics taxonomy):
* burst_admission_requests_total{pool, tenant, decision}
* burst_admission_denials_total{pool, tenant, reason_code}
* burst_pool_utilization_ratio{pool}
* burst_pool_queue_depth{pool} (if queueing enabled)
* dedicated_baseline_p95_latency_ms{pool, tenant}
* nonburst_neighbor_latency_delta{pool}
* telemetry_lag_seconds{source}

Cardinality constraints:
* Tenant label must be hashed or limited to top-N on shared dashboards.
* Per-tenant deep dives restricted to authorized roles.

h1. Dependencies

* Fleet capacity provisioning for burst pools by region/hardware class (Fleet/Infra).
* Admission controller service implementation and policy engine integration (Platform Control Plane).
* Smart routing integration (Routing).
* Metering pipeline updates and billing export (Quotas/Billing + Data).
* Console surfaces (Console team).
* SRE dashboards/alerts and runbooks (SRE/Observability).
* Legal review for contract language and SLA expectations (Legal).

h1. Rollout plan

h2. Phase 0: Discovery & design (2025-01 to 2025-02)
* Confirm customer requirements and peak patterns.
* Finalize pool sizing policy and initial region/hardware scope.
* Align on denial semantics, fairness model, circuit breakers.
* Pricing assumptions and packaging strawman.

Exit criteria:
* Approved design spec + ADRs.
* Draft contract language and initial pricing direction.

h2. Phase 1: Implementation (2025-03 to 2025-04)
* Control plane config APIs and validation.
* Admission controller service + fairness + cooldown.
* Routing integration and deterministic decision handling.
* Metering and billing export.
* Initial dashboards and alerting.

Exit criteria:
* End-to-end staging test with synthetic burst.
* Audit logging verified.
* Load testing shows no baseline regression.

h2. Phase 2: Internal beta / dogfood (2025-05)
* Enable for internal workloads + 3–5 friendly Dedicated accounts.
* Measure denial distributions, latency deltas, and metering correctness.
* Iterate on thresholds and alert noise.

Exit criteria:
* Metering reconciliation passes.
* No sustained neighbor SLO regression incidents.
* Support playbook validated with real tickets.

h2. Phase 3: GA staged rollout (2025-06)
* Roll out by region/hardware class with allow-list.
* Publish docs and CS/Sales enablement.
* Enforce SLO regression gate for expansion.

Exit criteria:
* Achieve stable metrics and acceptable denial rates for defined target customers.

h2. Phase 4: Post-GA hardening (2025-07 onward)
* Improve prediction signals and reduce denials caused by telemetry lag.
* Expand region/hardware coverage.
* Consider bounded queueing mode for batch workloads.

h1. Success metrics

h2. Customer value

* Reduced need for baseline over-provisioning: target >= 20% reduction in “requested baseline uplift for peak” among burst-enabled customers (measured via CS notes + contract deltas).
* Peak performance improvement: customers report meeting peak throughput targets without manual interventions in >= 80% of burst windows.

h2. Platform safety

* No statistically significant regression in non-burst tenants’ p95 latency during burst rollout (within agreed thresholds) across enabled pools.
* Circuit breaker effectiveness: during induced or real saturation events, mitigation reduces neighbor latency deltas to within threshold within 15 minutes.

h2. Operability

* Supportability: for > 90% of burst-related support tickets, Support/CS can identify primary denial reason and next action within 15 minutes using dashboards and denial reason codes.
* Alert quality: paging alerts maintain acceptable precision (target: < 1 false page per week per region/hardware pool after tuning period).

h2. Billing correctness

* Burst usage reconciliation: < 0.5% variance between metering export and internal ground truth sampling.
* Zero known double-charging issues on retries after GA (validated via idempotency keys and periodic audits).

h1. Risks and mitigations

* Risk: Noisy-neighbor tail latency amplification.
  * Mitigation: strict fleet safety caps, fairness enforcement, SLO regression gate, fast circuit breaker.

* Risk: Telemetry lag causes unfair denials or oscillation.
  * Mitigation: smoothing, safe-deny behavior, determinism guarantees scoped to telemetry freshness; alert on telemetry lag.

* Risk: Customer confusion about “Dedicated” vs “Burst best-effort.”
  * Mitigation: crisp docs, explicit denial semantics, Console indicators, contract language.

* Risk: Metering accuracy issues (retries, partial failures).
  * Mitigation: idempotency keys, reconciliation jobs, billing holdback during beta, Finance review before GA.

* Risk: Insufficient pool capacity in constrained regions.
  * Mitigation: region/hardware allow-list at GA; procurement-aligned sizing policy; explicit availability statements.

h1. Open questions

1) Default max_burst_ratio for GA: propose 1.5x baseline; confirm with Fleet safety modeling.
2) Should we support customer-configurable cooldown vs policy-enforced only?
3) Final selection of HTTP status codes and error formats for SDK compatibility.
4) Which latency metrics are safe to show customers without misinterpretation (baseline vs burst windows)?
5) Priority tiers: how to communicate “Gold” without implying guaranteed burst availability?

h1. Approvals

* Product: Lauren Bishop (owner)
* Control Plane: Ethan Park
* Fleet/Infra: Vanessa Ortiz
* SRE: Sean Gallagher
* Finance: Rishi Malhotra / Laura Bennett
* Legal: Michael Grant / Sofia Mendes

(Approval sign-offs tracked in PM ticket PM-512 and pricing ticket PM-528.)

text:
h1. Summary

Dedicated Burst Capacity is an add-on option for Redwood Dedicated customers that allows *temporary throughput above a tenant’s reserved (baseline) GPU allocation* by borrowing from Redwood-managed *burst pools* (shared, segmented by region and hardware class). Bursting is strictly controlled via admission control, fairness enforcement, cooldown windows, and fleet-wide circuit breakers to prevent noisy-neighbor effects and protect platform-wide SLOs.

This PRD defines the customer problem, user stories, requirements, non-goals, SLOs, pricing assumptions, rollout plan, and success metrics.

h1. Problem statement

Dedicated customers routinely experience predictable peaks (e.g., batch re-indexing, weekly traffic spikes, product launches). Today, they must either:

* Over-provision baseline Dedicated capacity (wasting cost most of the time), or
* Accept degraded performance and/or hard throttling during peaks.

Customers want a way to *pay for occasional headroom* without permanently increasing committed baseline capacity, while still receiving a “Dedicated-grade” experience (reliable, isolated, supportable).

h1. Goals

* Provide a *paid* burst option for Dedicated customers that increases throughput during predictable peaks without requiring permanent over-provisioning.
* Protect non-bursting tenants and the broader fleet from noisy-neighbor effects via strict admission control, fairness, and circuit breakers.
* Make bursting observable, auditable, and supportable:
  * customer-visible reporting and Console surfaces
  * clear denial reasons
  * SRE alerts and operational runbooks
* Integrate with billing and packaging:
  * accurate burst metering
  * configurable per-tenant limits
  * contract/SLA language that sets correct expectations

h1. Non-goals (initial release)

* Cross-region bursting (burst capacity is constrained within a region).
* Customer-custom burst pools (initially shared Redwood-managed pools with segmentation).
* Spot-market behavior, auction pricing, or reservation trading.
* Guaranteeing burst availability at all times (burst is “best-effort within guardrails,” with clear limits and denial semantics).

h1. Definitions

* *Baseline (Reserved) Capacity:* Dedicated GPUs committed to the tenant (contracted, reserved pools).
* *Burst Capacity:* Additional capacity above baseline, sourced from Redwood-managed burst pools.
* *Burst Pool:* A shared pool of GPUs segmented by (region, hardware class) with fleet safety caps.
* *Burst Window:* The time interval during which a tenant is actively consuming burst capacity.
* *Admission Controller:* Data-plane-adjacent service that evaluates whether a request can consume burst capacity now, returning an admit/deny decision with reason.

h1. Personas

* *ML/Platform Engineer (Customer):* owns throughput and latency; needs predictable scaling behavior and clear operational knobs.
* *SRE/On-call (Redwood):* needs guardrails, alerts, and safe rollback to protect non-burst SLOs.
* *Account Executive / Solutions Engineer:* needs a crisp story for “burst vs increasing baseline,” with packaging boundaries and expectation setting.
* *Finance/Procurement (Customer):* wants a clear bill line item, predictable caps, and auditability.

h1. Key user stories

1) *Burst enablement and limits*
* As a Dedicated customer admin, I can enable burst for my tenant and set a maximum burst limit so we can safely handle peaks without runaway spend.

2) *Peak throughput handling*
* As an application owner, during a predictable spike, I can exceed baseline throughput while still receiving clear feedback if burst is unavailable or capped.

3) *Operational visibility*
* As a platform engineer, I can see burst usage over time, current limit state, denial reasons, and latency impact so I can tune my client behavior and capacity strategy.

4) *Redwood safety and support*
* As Redwood SRE, I can detect burst pool saturation and neighbor impact early, and quickly mitigate via circuit breakers/limit reductions with auditable actions.

5) *Billing clarity*
* As customer finance, I can reconcile burst charges to measured burst usage, with no double counting on retries.

h1. Product requirements

h2. Functional requirements

h3. Burst pool model

* Burst pools are provisioned and managed by Redwood by:
  * region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
  * hardware class (e.g., H100, L40S)
* A Dedicated tenant may burst only into pools that match its allowed region/hardware class.
* Burst pools must support logical segmentation to reduce correlated failures and prevent “one big shared pool” risk.

h3. Tenant configuration

The control plane must support per-tenant burst configuration:

|| Field || Description || Example ||
| burst_enabled | Master enablement toggle | true |
| max_burst_ratio | Maximum burst relative to baseline (cap) | 1.5 (i.e., up to +50% of baseline) |
| max_burst_gpus | Absolute cap for burst GPUs (optional override) | 8 |
| burst_cooldown_seconds | Cooldown after sustained bursting or after cap reached | 900 |
| priority_tier | Admission priority tier for fairness weights | silver |
| allowed_regions | Burst allowed regions (subset) | [us-east] |
| allowed_hardware_classes | Allowed hardware classes | [H100] |

Validation requirements:
* Must not allow burst caps that exceed fleet safety policy by default.
* Safe defaults: burst disabled unless explicitly enabled.
* Changes are auditable (see Audit Logging).

h3. Admission control & denial semantics

* Every request that would exceed baseline capacity must be evaluated for burst admission.
* Admission decisions must be *deterministic for a given request context* to avoid oscillation (within the limits of telemetry freshness).
* Denial must return a *machine-readable reason code* and a recommended client action.

Denial behaviors (initial release):
* Immediate deny with backpressure response for synchronous API traffic.
* Optional bounded queueing may be enabled for specific workloads later; not required for GA.

h3. Fairness and anti-starvation

* Admission must enforce fairness across bursting tenants within the same pool.
* Must include anti-starvation protections so one high-priority tenant cannot permanently suppress others.
* Must provide SRE-visible metrics that quantify fairness outcomes (e.g., per-tenant admitted share vs configured weights).

h3. Cooldown and burst shaping

* Enforce a cooldown window to prevent repeated “burst hammering” that destabilizes pools.
* Allow a configured “ramp-up” behavior (soft start) to reduce sudden tail latency amplification.

h3. Routing and fallback behavior

When burst is denied:
* Routing must not silently push the request into other tenants’ baseline capacity.
* Supported actions (policy-based):
  1) Return backpressure (429 / overload) with reason + retry hints
  2) Route to baseline-only path (slower/queued) if customer has chosen this mode
  3) Fallback to compatible model variant within the tenant’s baseline (only if configured by customer and compatible with their quality requirements)

h3. Metering and billing hooks

* Meter burst usage accurately and export to billing pipeline.
* Must be idempotent on retries (no double counting).
* Provide per-tenant daily summary exports for CS and customers.

h3. Console / customer experience

In Redwood Console for Dedicated tenants:
* Show current burst status (enabled/disabled), configured limits, and priority tier.
* Show usage charts:
  * baseline utilization vs burst utilization
  * burst “headroom remaining” indicators
* Show denial breakdown:
  * by reason code
  * over time
* Provide guidance text:
  * what to do when denied
  * recommended retry/backoff patterns

h3. Audit logging

* Log all burst configuration changes, including:
  * enable/disable
  * limit changes
  * priority tier changes
  * admin overrides / emergency actions
* Audit entries must include actor, timestamp, before/after, and justification (for manual overrides).

h2. Non-functional requirements

h3. Reliability / safety

* Primary safety requirement: *do not regress non-burst tenants’ SLOs.*
* Must implement fleet-wide safety caps and a global circuit breaker that can rapidly reduce/disable burst.
* Must support staged rollout and fast rollback.

h3. Performance

* Admission check must add minimal overhead. Target: p99 admission evaluation time <= 5 ms in-region.
* Telemetry lag must not cause systematic unfair denials; system must behave safely under partial telemetry outage.

h3. Security

* RBAC: only authorized roles can modify burst configuration.
* Debug tooling must redact sensitive identifiers when used outside trusted contexts.

h3. Operability

* On-call must have:
  * dashboards for saturation, denials, latency deltas
  * clear runbooks
  * tooling to explain denial decisions for a given request/tenant

h1. SLOs and SLAs

h2. What is protected

For GA, Redwood protects the *baseline Dedicated experience*.

* Baseline capacity SLOs remain the primary commitment.
* Burst capacity is provided on a controlled, guardrailed basis and may be denied to protect fleet safety and neighbor SLOs.

h2. Proposed SLO targets (initial)

|| Metric || Applies to || Target || Notes ||
| Dedicated baseline availability | Dedicated tenants | >= 99.9% monthly | Existing Dedicated SLO; bursting must not degrade it |
| Dedicated baseline latency | Dedicated tenants | No regression vs pre-burst baseline | Enforced via SLO regression gate |
| Burst admission service availability | Burst-enabled tenants | >= 99.9% monthly | If admission service is unhealthy, default to safe deny/disable burst |
| Burst admission overhead | Burst path | p99 <= 5 ms | Measured at gateway/routing layer |

h2. Guardrail SLO gates

* If non-burst tenant p95 latency in a pool increases by more than agreed threshold (see design spec; initial: +10% sustained for 10 minutes) AND correlates with burst saturation, automatically:
  * reduce max burst ratio globally for that pool, or
  * disable burst for that pool (circuit breaker)
* Error budget gates for rollout:
  * do not expand rollout if baseline SLO burn rate exceeds policy thresholds

h1. Packaging & pricing assumptions (for PRD; final owned by Pricing/Packaging doc)

Note: numbers here are placeholders for modeling and UX; final pricing requires Finance approval and GTM alignment.

h2. Packaging proposal

* Burst is an add-on for Dedicated contracts.
* Default availability: selected regions/hardware classes at GA.
* Priority tiers (example):
  * *Silver:* standard fairness weight, standard caps
  * *Gold:* higher fairness weight within strict anti-starvation constraints

h2. Metering unit

* Bill burst usage as *burst GPU-seconds* (or equivalent normalized compute-seconds) attributable to burst-only work.
* Secondary reporting for customer comprehension:
  * burst tokens generated
  * burst request counts

h2. Pricing assumptions

* Burst usage is charged at a premium vs baseline committed rate to reflect on-demand flexibility and fleet risk.
* Contracts include:
  * a maximum burst cap (ratio and/or absolute GPUs)
  * a statement that burst is subject to admission control and may be denied to protect fleet safety

h1. UX and API behavior

h2. Customer-visible responses when burst is denied

When burst is denied, customers must receive:
* HTTP status: 429 (Overloaded) or equivalent backpressure error
* Response body includes:
  * reason_code
  * retry_after_ms (when applicable)
  * guidance string (short)

Initial reason codes (must be stable and documented):

|| reason_code || Meaning || Recommended client behavior ||
| BURST_DISABLED | Burst not enabled for tenant/region/hw | Use baseline capacity; request config change if needed |
| TENANT_BURST_CAP_REACHED | Tenant hit configured burst cap | Backoff; reduce concurrency; wait for cooldown |
| POOL_SATURATED | Pool at or above safe utilization | Exponential backoff + jitter; consider baseline increase |
| FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN | Global breaker open for pool | Retry with backoff; expect limited availability until cleared |
| TELEMETRY_STALE_SAFE_DENY | Telemetry too stale; safe deny | Retry with backoff; Redwood investigating if sustained |
| POLICY_VALIDATION_FAILED | Misconfig (invalid limits/region mismatch) | Contact Support; fix config |

h1. Observability requirements

h2. Customer-facing

Console dashboards must include:
* Burst usage over time (GPU-seconds, tokens, requests)
* Current configured cap vs current estimated consumption
* Denial reasons over time
* “Burst impact” indicators (customer-visible but carefully framed):
  * p95 latency (baseline vs during burst windows)
  * admission latency

h2. Internal SRE/Engineering

Required metrics (examples; final names in metrics taxonomy):
* burst_admission_requests_total{pool, tenant, decision}
* burst_admission_denials_total{pool, tenant, reason_code}
* burst_pool_utilization_ratio{pool}
* burst_pool_queue_depth{pool} (if queueing enabled)
* dedicated_baseline_p95_latency_ms{pool, tenant}
* nonburst_neighbor_latency_delta{pool}
* telemetry_lag_seconds{source}

Cardinality constraints:
* Tenant label must be hashed or limited to top-N on shared dashboards.
* Per-tenant deep dives restricted to authorized roles.

h1. Dependencies

* Fleet capacity provisioning for burst pools by region/hardware class (Fleet/Infra).
* Admission controller service implementation and policy engine integration (Platform Control Plane).
* Smart routing integration (Routing).
